title: Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT)
description: Understand AMT calculation methodology and apply strategies to minimize AMT exposure.
topics: Identify common AMT preference items and adjustments
Apply strategies to minimize AMT exposure
Analyze ISO exercise timing for AMT impact

sections.title: AMT Overview
sections.content: The Alternative Minimum Tax is a parallel tax system designed to ensure high-income taxpayers pay at least a minimum amount of tax.

sections.title: Exemption Phase-out
sections.content: Phase-out: 25% of AMTI above threshold. The exemption is reduced by 25 cents for every dollar above the phase-out threshold.

sections.title: AMT Preference Items
rows: Tax-exempt for regular tax, taxable for AMT (exceptions apply)
Bargain element at exercise: (FMV - Exercise Price) × Shares
Difference vs. straight-line, primarily affects real property

sections.title: The ISO AMT Trap
sections.content: Example: Exercise 10,000 shares at $10 exercise price when FMV is $50. Bargain element: $40 × 10,000 = $400,000 AMT income! This can trigger significant unexpected AMT liability.

sections.title: ISO Strategies
items: Consider early exercise when spread is small

sections.title: AMT Credit
sections.content: When you pay AMT due to timing differences (like ISOs), you get an AMT credit. It carries forward indefinitely, is used when regular tax exceeds TMT, and has a refundable portion for some taxpayers.

sections.title: AMT Planning Strategies
rows: Income already taxed at 26-28%; additional STCG taxed at same rate vs. 32-37%
If AMT income is low enough; push income to regular tax year
Large SALT payments don't help in AMT years; consider deferring to non-AMT year

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: AMT is a parallel tax system ensuring minimum tax payment
Key adjustments: SALT add-back, home equity interest
Key preferences: ISO exercise, private activity bonds
ISO bargain element creates AMT income at exercise
AMT credit carries forward for timing differences

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following best describes the fundamental approach to calculating Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT)?
options: Calculate regular tax liability, then subtract the AMT exemption amount.
Calculate regular taxable income, add back certain preference items and adjustments, calculate AMT liability, and pay the higher of regular tax or AMT.
Calculate regular tax liability, then add back certain preference items and adjustments to determine AMT liability.
Calculate AMT liability first, then use that as the basis for calculating regular tax liability.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The AMT calculation starts with regular taxable income, adds back preference items and adjustments to arrive at AMT income, calculates AMT liability, and then compares it to the regular tax liability. The taxpayer pays the higher of the two.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the purpose of AMT – to ensure a minimum level of tax is paid.

sections.title: Common AMT Preference Items and Adjustments

reveal.question: Name at least three common items that can trigger or increase AMT liability.
reveal.answer: Common AMT preference items and adjustments include: 1) State and local taxes (limited deduction under TCJA, but still an adjustment in some cases); 2) Incentive Stock Options (ISO) exercise (the difference between the market price and the exercise price is an AMT adjustment in the year of exercise); 3) Depreciation (accelerated depreciation methods used for regular tax may be adjusted for AMT purposes); 4) Tax-exempt interest from private activity bonds.

knowledgeCheck.question: A client exercises Incentive Stock Options (ISOs). For Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT) purposes, what is the impact in the year of exercise?
options: The difference between the fair market value of the stock and the exercise price is included as income for regular tax purposes.
The difference between the fair market value of the stock and the exercise price is an AMT adjustment.
The exercise of ISOs has no impact on either regular tax or AMT in the year of exercise.
The amount paid for the options is deductible for AMT purposes.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The difference between the fair market value and the exercise price is *not* included in income for regular tax in the year of exercise. However, it *is* an AMT adjustment. This can create a situation where the client owes AMT even if they don't sell the stock.
knowledgeCheck.hint: ISOs receive special tax treatment, but this can create AMT complications.

rows.label: Tax Base
values: Regular Taxable Income
Alternative Minimum Taxable Income (AMTI)

rows.label: Standard Deduction
values: Allowed
Not Allowed

rows.label: State and Local Taxes (SALT)
values: Limited deduction ($10,000 limit)
Not Deductible

rows.label: Tax Rates
values: Progressive tax rates
Two-tiered rate structure (26% and 28%)

title: Passive Activity and At-Risk Rules
description: Distinguish passive from non-passive activities and apply material participation tests.

sections.title: Overview of Loss Limitation Rules
sections.content: Three loss limitation rules apply in order: 1) Basis Limitation (How much in the game), 2) At-Risk Rules (How much you can lose), 3) Passive Activity Rules (Nature of the activity).

sections.title: Passive Activity Rules (§469)
sections.content: Passive Activities Defined: 1) Trade or business where taxpayer does NOT materially participate, 2) Rental activities (generally passive by definition). Key Rule: Passive losses can only offset passive income.

sections.title: Suspended Losses
sections.content: Suspended losses carry forward until the activity becomes profitable or the activity is fully disposed.

sections.title: Material Participation Tests
rows: 100+ hours AND no one participates more

sections.title: Material Participation Mnemonic
sections.content: 500, All, 100+, SPA5-3, Regular - These represent the key tests to remember for material participation.

sections.title: $25,000 Rental Allowance
sections.content: For active participation (lower standard than material): Deduct up to $25,000 passive losses against ordinary income. Must own at least 10% of activity. Applies to rental real estate only.

sections.title: Phase-out Formula
sections.content: Allowance Reduction = (AGI - $100,000) × 50%

sections.title: Real Estate Professional Exception
sections.content: Requirements (Both Must Be Met): 1) More than 750 hours in real property trades/businesses, 2) More than 50% of personal service time in real property activities. Additional requirement: Must materially participate in each rental OR elect to aggregate all rentals as one activity.

sections.title: Real Estate Professional Result
sections.content: Rental losses are NOT passive — they become fully deductible against ordinary income. This is a powerful tax planning tool for qualifying professionals.

sections.title: Disposition of Passive Activities
sections.content: Upon complete disposition to unrelated party: All suspended losses become deductible, includes losses from prior years, activity must be fully terminated.

sections.title: Types of Dispositions
rows: Losses lost, but basis stepped up

sections.title: Planning Strategies
items: Passive income generators (PIGs): Create passive income to absorb passive losses - rental income, K-1 income
Group activities: Combine related activities for easier material participation
Real estate professional status: Must genuinely meet requirements, documentation is critical
Timing dispositions: Trigger suspended losses strategically

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Passive losses only offset passive income
Material participation: 500+ hours is the clearest test
Rentals get $25,000 allowance with active participation
Real estate professionals can deduct rental losses fully
Complete disposition releases suspended losses

sections.title: Passive Activity Loss Limitation: Knowledge Check

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following loss limitation rules is applied *last* when determining the deductibility of losses from a business activity?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The passive activity rules are applied *after* the basis and at-risk rules. This ensures that the taxpayer has sufficient basis and is at risk for the loss before considering whether the activity is passive.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the order in which the IRS assesses risk and potential for abuse.

reveal.question: Name three of the seven material participation tests used to determine if an activity is considered passive.
reveal.answer: Any three of the following: (1) More than 500 hours; (2) Substantially all participation; (3) More than 100 hours and no one else more; (4) Significant participation activities exceeding 500 hours; (5) Material participation in any 5 of the past 10 years; (6) Material participation in any prior year for a personal service activity; (7) Based on all the facts and circumstances, the individual participated on a regular, continuous, and substantial basis during the year.

sections.title: Distinguishing At-Risk and Passive Activity Rules

rows.label: Focus
values: Amount the taxpayer can *actually* lose in an activity.
Nature of the activity (passive vs. active).

rows.label: Primary Limitation
values: Amount of cash and the adjusted basis of property the taxpayer has invested.
Losses are deductible only to the extent of passive income.

rows.label: Carryover of Disallowed Losses
values: Carried forward indefinitely until sufficient at-risk basis exists.
Carried forward indefinitely until passive income is generated or the activity is disposed of in a fully taxable transaction.

title: Section 199A Qualified Business Income Deduction
description: Calculate the QBI deduction and navigate threshold and phase-out rules.
topics: Calculate the QBI deduction for eligible taxpayers
Apply W-2 wages and capital limitations
Identify specified service trades or businesses (SSTBs)

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: The Qualified Business Income deduction allows eligible taxpayers to deduct up to 20% of qualified business income from pass-through entities.

sections.title: NOT Eligible
sections.content: C corporations and W-2 wages (from employment) are NOT eligible for the QBI deduction.

sections.title: Basic Calculation
sections.content: QBI Deduction = Lesser of: 1) 20% of QBI, OR 2) 20% of (Taxable Income - Net Capital Gains)

sections.title: W-2 Wages and Capital Limitation
sections.content: Above the threshold, QBI deduction is limited to the GREATER of: Option 1: 50% of W-2 wages, or Option 2: 25% of W-2 wages + 2.5% of UBIA (Unadjusted Basis Immediately After Acquisition).

sections.title: Specified Service Trades or Businesses (SSTBs)
sections.content: SSTBs Face Additional Limits: Above threshold, NO 199A deduction is allowed.

sections.title: Phase-In Calculations
sections.content: For income in the phase-in range: Non-SSTBs have wage/capital limitation phase in. SSTBs have both QBI and wages/capital reduced. Applicable % formula uses (Taxable Income - Threshold) / $50,000 (or $100,000 MFJ).

sections.title: Aggregation Rules
sections.content: Taxpayers may elect to aggregate businesses if: Same owner (directly or indirectly), Same tax year, and meet two of three factors: 1) Same products/services, 2) Shared facilities/operations, 3) Common ownership (50%+). Benefit: Combine high-wage and high-capital businesses.

sections.title: Planning Strategies
rows: Pay yourself more salary from S corp - more wages = higher deduction ceiling
Acquire capital-intensive businesses, consider timing of asset purchases
Structure business to avoid SSTB, separate non-SSTB activities
Keep income below thresholds, time income recognition, consider Roth conversions strategically

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: QBI deduction = 20% of qualified business income
W-2/UBIA limits apply above income thresholds
SSTBs get no deduction above thresholds
Aggregation can help maximize the deduction
Deduction expires after 2025 without legislation

knowledgeCheck.question: Sarah, a single taxpayer, owns a landscaping business (not an SSTB). Her taxable income before the QBI deduction is $200,000, and her qualified business income (QBI) is $120,000. What is the maximum QBI deduction Sarah can take?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The QBI deduction is the LESSER of 20% of QBI or 20% of taxable income. 20% of QBI is $24,000 (0.20 * $120,000). 20% of taxable income is $40,000 (0.20 * $200,000). Therefore, the deduction is $24,000.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to calculate both 20% of QBI and 20% of taxable income and choose the smaller amount.

reveal.question: What is the 2024 taxable income threshold at which the QBI deduction for a Specified Service Trade or Business (SSTB) begins to be phased out for single filers?
reveal.answer: $191,950. Above this amount, the QBI deduction for SSTBs begins to be phased out. It is fully phased out once taxable income exceeds $241,950.

headers: Taxable Income Above Threshold (But Within Phase-out Range)

rows.label: SSTB QBI Deduction
values: Full QBI deduction allowed (subject to the 20% of QBI and 20% of taxable income limitations)
QBI deduction is limited based on a complex formula involving W-2 wages and capital limitations. The deduction is reduced, potentially to zero, as income increases within the phase-out range.

rows.label: Non-SSTB QBI Deduction
values: Full QBI deduction allowed (subject to the 20% of QBI and 20% of taxable income limitations)
QBI deduction is subject to W-2 wage and capital limitations, but the deduction isn't automatically reduced to zero. The limitations are applied to the full QBI deduction amount.

title: Net Investment Income Tax and Additional Medicare Tax
description: Calculate the 3.8% NIIT on investment income and develop strategies to minimize surtaxes.
topics: Calculate the 3.8% NIIT on investment income
Identify income included and excluded from NIIT
Develop strategies to minimize these surtaxes

sections.title: NIIT Calculation
sections.content: NIIT = 3.8% × Lesser of: Net Investment Income, OR MAGI - Threshold

sections.title: Net Investment Income Excludes
items: S corp/partner income with material participation

sections.title: NIIT Calculation Example
sections.content: MFJ with MAGI: $320,000. Net Investment Income: $50,000. Excess MAGI: $320,000 - $250,000 = $70,000. Lesser of NII ($50,000) or excess ($70,000) = $50,000. NIIT: 3.8% × $50,000 = $1,900.

sections.title: Additional Medicare Tax (§3101(b)(2))
sections.content: Applies to wages over threshold and self-employment income over threshold. Calculation: 0.9% × (Earned Income - Threshold).

sections.title: Medicare Tax Withholding Issue
sections.content: Employers must withhold starting at $200,000 regardless of filing status. MFJ may underwithhold (threshold $250K). MFS may underwithhold (threshold $125K).

sections.title: Self-Employed Medicare Tax
sections.content: For SE income: Regular SE tax is 15.3% (12.4% + 2.9%). Additional 0.9% applies on excess over threshold. Only 50% of regular SE tax is deductible.

sections.title: Combined Tax Rates for High-Income Taxpayers
Rate Notes
sections.content: *Plus 6.2% employee FICA if below wage base

sections.title: Planning Strategies
rows: Maximize retirement contributions, harvest capital losses, time income recognition, municipal bond investments
Active business income (materially participate), qualified retirement plan distributions, Roth conversions (planned)
Rental income becomes non-passive, escapes NIIT classification
Spread gain recognition, may keep MAGI below threshold
Reduces NII directly, reduces MAGI (if gains absorbed)

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: NIIT: 3.8% on investment income above $200K/$250K
Add Medicare: 0.9% on wages/SE above $200K/$250K
NIIT applies to capital gains, dividends, interest, passive income
Active business income and muni bonds escape NIIT
Top combined rate on investment income: 23.8%

knowledgeCheck.question: John and Mary are married filing jointly. John earns $180,000 in wages, and Mary earns $60,000 in wages. They also have $30,000 in net investment income. What is the amount of their Net Investment Income Tax (NIIT)?
knowledgeCheck.explanation: The NIIT threshold for married filing jointly is $250,000. Their modified adjusted gross income (MAGI) is $240,000 ($180,000 + $60,000), so the excess is $0. The tax base is the lesser of (a) net investment income ($30,000) or (b) the excess of MAGI over the threshold. Since MAGI is below the threshold, the excess is $0. But, we must still calculate the NIIT as if the threshold was exceeded to determine if it is less than the net investment income. The excess of MAGI over the threshold would be considered $240,000 - $250,000 = -$10,000. Since NIIT is the lesser of (a) net investment income ($30,000) or (b) the excess of MAGI over the threshold ($0), the NIIT is calculated on $30,000. $30,000 * 0.03 = $900.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Remember to compare net investment income to the amount that MAGI exceeds the threshold.

reveal.question: What are the income thresholds for the Additional Medicare Tax for Single, Married Filing Jointly, and Married Filing Separately filers?
reveal.answer: Single: $200,000; Married Filing Jointly: $250,000; Married Filing Separately: $125,000. Note that these thresholds are *not* indexed for inflation.

rows.label: Income Subject To Tax
values: Net Investment Income (e.g., interest, dividends, capital gains, rental and royalty income)
Wages, self-employment income, and other compensation

rows.label: Income Thresholds (Single)
values: MAGI exceeding $200,000
Wages exceeding $200,000

rows.label: Employer Responsibility
values: None
Employers are required to withhold Additional Medicare Tax from wages exceeding $200,000 (regardless of filing status)
